mappings.rationale: Directly covers propositional calculus, predicate calculus, modal logic, and applied logic including logical fallacies, which are the core topics of this section.
Section B.3 covers completeness, consistency, decidability, and undecidability in formal mathematical systems, which are the direct subject of Gödel's theorems.
Set theory, formal systems, and the foundations connecting mathematics to logic are covered in sections A.5 and B of this section.
Russell's work on the theory of types, Principia Mathematica, and the foundations of logic is directly relevant to metalogic and formal systems in sections A and B.
Set theory and natural-number arithmetic (section A.5) deal with infinite sets and cardinality, which are central topics in the study of infinity.
Wittgenstein's Tractatus made foundational contributions to the propositional calculus and the philosophy of formal languages discussed in sections A.1 and B.
Analytic philosophy is deeply rooted in formal logic, model theory, and metalogical studies, all core concerns of this section.
Section A.3 covers syllogistic logic, which is Aristotle's foundational contribution to formal logic, the theory of the syllogism.
Section C.1.a covers the theory of argumentation and the new rhetoric, which is directly addressed by the study of rhetoric.
Leibniz pioneered the idea of a calculus of reasoning and symbolic logic, directly relevant to the formal calculi discussed in sections A and B.
Applied logic (section C) and metalogic connect to computability theory, and epistemic and practical logics have applications in computer science.
Applied logic including epistemic logic (C.2) and practical logic (C.3) dealing with knowledge, belief, and planning are foundational to artificial intelligence.
Section A.5 covers set theory and natural-number arithmetic, which connects to number-theoretic foundations used in metalogical proofs.
Epistemic logic (section C.2) dealing with belief, knowledge, and assertion connects to the philosophical study of information.
Section C.2 covers epistemic logic, which deals with the formal logic of belief, knowledge, assertion, and doubt.
Section C.4 covers logics of physical application, connecting formal logic to the philosophy and methodology of physical sciences.
